title: Chess Piece Detection & Classification
description: The project focuses on detecting and classifying chess pieces using AI and computer vision techniques to convert physical chess positions into a digital format.

sections.title: Introduction
content: The project aims to create a system that can detect and classify chess pieces arranged on a board using a neural network.
The approach leverages advanced digital image processing techniques to identify the precise locations of each piece on the board.
The final output is represented in Forsyth-Edwards Notation (FEN), a widely accepted format for describing chess positions.

sections.title: Background and Motivation
content: Chess is a domain where artificial intelligence (AI) has a longstanding history, dating back to the early attempts of building chess-playing programs.
This project contributes to that field by addressing the challenge of converting physical chess positions into digital formats accurately.
Through the application of deep learning and computer vision, this solution not only serves chess enthusiasts but also advances AI's ability to interpret real-world scenarios.

sections.title: Methodology
content: ### Data Collection
A proprietary dataset was developed, consisting of over 100 images of chessboards with different configurations of pieces.
The images were manually labeled, providing the necessary ground truth for training and evaluating the model.
The dataset accounted for various lighting conditions to enhance the system's robustness in real-world scenarios.
### Preprocessing
1. Grayscale Conversion: The original color images were converted to grayscale to simplify the processing.
2. Gaussian Blurring: Applied to reduce noise and improve edge detection.
3. Edge Detection: Canny edge detection was used to identify prominent boundaries in the image.
4. Hough Transformation: This technique detected the grid lines on the chessboard, allowing for segmentation of the board into individual cells.
### Model Training
The project utilized the YOLOv8 architecture, a state-of-the-art object detection framework.
The training process involved multiple iterations using a k-fold cross-validation strategy to ensure the model's generalization across different data splits.
Hyperparameters like learning rate and batch size were tuned to optimize the model's performance.

sections.title: Results and Evaluation
content: The system's effectiveness was measured using metrics such as precision, recall, and F1 score.
Tests on different lighting conditions demonstrated the model's robustness, achieving high accuracy in detecting and classifying chess pieces under various scenarios.
Validation results showed consistent F1 scores across all folds of the cross-validation process, confirming the reliability of the approach.
### Confusion Matrices
Confusion matrices for different folds indicated that certain types of pieces (e.g., pawns) were more challenging to classify accurately compared to others like rooks or queens.
This insight led to further adjustments in the training data to balance the representation of piece types.

sections.title: Conclusion
content: This project successfully developed a system capable of digitizing chess positions from physical boards.
The integration of deep learning with traditional image processing techniques provided a comprehensive solution for real-time chess position detection.
Future work will explore enhancing the model's accuracy further and expanding the dataset to include more varied board configurations and piece designs.

sections.title: Keywords
content: Chess
Deep Learning
Artificial Intelligence
Computer Vision
FEN
